perturbation.original_text: 1.3 Party A shall provide, for a consideration, network resources, portals and such other advertising resources, as well as connection services and business fee calculation/collection service. Party A will charge relevant information fee from the users of Party B's mobile game service, and will share such information fee with Party B as agreed herein.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.3 Party A shall provide, without consideration, network resources, portals and such other advertising resources, as well as connection services and business fee calculation/collection service. Party A will charge relevant information fee from the users of Party B's mobile game service, and will share such information fee with Party B as agreed herein.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "for a consideration" to "without consideration" creates a contradiction because it implies that Party A is providing services for free, which is not commercially reasonable. This could be interpreted as an illegal gift, especially if the value of the services is significant.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 15 U.S. Code § 1 - Contracts, combinations, and conspiracies in restraint of trade
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification could be viewed as a violation of antitrust laws, specifically Section 1 of the Sherman Act (15 U.S.C. § 1). If Party A provides network resources without consideration, it might be seen as an attempt to unfairly dominate the market, giving an unfair advantage to Party B. This could potentially restrain trade by limiting competition.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 1 - Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1 - Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty U.S. Code Notes prev | next Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal. Every person who shall make any contract or engage in any combination or conspiracy hereby declared to be illegal shall be deemed guilty of a felony, and, on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding $100,000,000 if a corporation, or, if any other person, $1,000,000, or by imprisonment not exceeding 10 years, or by both said punishments, in the discretion of the court. (July 2, 1890, ch. 647, § 1, 26 Stat. 209 ; Aug. 17, 1937, ch. 690, title VIII, 50 Stat. 693 ; July 7, 1955, ch. 281, 69 Stat. 282 ; Pub. L. 93–528, § 3 , Dec. 21, 1974 , 88 Stat. 1708 ; Pub. L. 94–145, § 2 , Dec. 12, 1975 , 89 Stat. 801 ; Pub. L. 101–588, § 4(a) , Nov. 16, 1990 , 104 Stat. 2880 ; Pub. L. 108–237, title II, § 215(a) , June 22, 2004 , 118 Stat. 668 .) L. 101–588 substituted “$10,000,000” for “one million dollars” and “$350,000” for “one hundred thousand dollars”. 1975— Pub. L. 94–145 struck out from first sentence two provisos granting anti-trust exemption to State fair trade laws. 1974— Pub. L. 93–528 substituted “a felony, and, on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding one million dollars if a corporation, or, if any other person, one hundred thousand dollars, or by imprisonment not exceeding three years” for “a misdemeanor, and on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding f
perturbation.justification: The modification of the contract, by changing "for a consideration" to "without consideration," creates a contradiction regarding the provision of services. According to the LLM's explanation, this could potentially violate antitrust laws, specifically Section 1 of the Sherman Act (15 U.S.C. § 1). Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 both refer to 15 U.S.C. § 1, which declares illegal "every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce." While the snippet does not directly state that providing services without consideration is illegal, the LLM explanation suggests it could be construed as an unfair advantage, potentially restraining trade. The altered term directly creates a situation that raises antitrust concerns, therefore creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.3 Party B must strictly comply with relevant industry laws, rules and standards promulgated by the State, including, without limitation, the PRC Telecommunications Regulations, Administrative Measures on Internet Information Service, Interim Provisions on the Administration of Internet Publishing, Interim Provisions on the Administration of Internet Websites' Engaging in News Publication Services, and such other laws, regulations, policies and industry management rules, and shall not distribute and disseminate illegal, unhealthy, reactionary information or advertisement or such other junk information on the Internet.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.3 Party B must loosely comply with relevant industry laws, rules and standards promulgated by the State, including, without limitation, the PRC Telecommunications Regulations, Administrative Measures on Internet Information Service, Interim Provisions on the Administration of Internet Publishing, Interim Provisions on the Administration of Internet Websites' Engaging in News Publication Services, and such other laws, regulations, policies and industry management rules, and may distribute and disseminate illegal, unhealthy, reactionary information or advertisement or such other junk information on the Internet.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "strictly comply" to "loosely comply" and the negation "shall not distribute" to the affirmation "may distribute" contradicts the legal requirement for compliance with regulations and prohibitions on disseminating illegal content. It creates uncertainty about the level of compliance required.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 中华人民共和国电信条例 (PRC Telecommunications Regulations)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text directly contradicts Article 57 of the PRC Telecommunications Regulations, which prohibits the use of telecommunications networks to transmit illegal content and mandates compliance with relevant laws and regulations. This change allows for the dissemination of illegal content and undermines the regulatory framework.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates 'strictly comply' and prohibits dissemination of illegal content, aligning with standard legal expectations. The changed text, by allowing 'loosely comply' and permitting dissemination of illegal content, directly opposes the legal intent to enforce industry standards and prevent the spread of unlawful information. The law explanation also confirms that the change contradicts Article 57 of the PRC Telecommunications Regulations. While scraped law snippet 1 discusses copyright, it also highlights the importance of using official Chinese-language versions as the final authority, indirectly reinforcing the need for strict adherence to regulations rather than loose interpretations. The alteration fundamentally undermines the regulatory framework intended to prevent the spread of illegal information.

perturbation.original_text: 7.2 The successful charging bill collected on Party A's billing system shall be used by the Parties as the basis for settlement of information fees; the business billed by time shall be calculated based on the successful receipt of the business by the customer; business subject to monthly payment shall be based on customer's true ordering. Party A shall deduct from the payable information fee, the amount of information fee refused to be paid by any users due to Party B's service quality.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.2 The charging bill collected on Party A's billing system, regardless of success, shall be used by the Parties as the basis for settlement of information fees; the business billed by time shall be calculated regardless of successful receipt of the business by the customer; business subject to monthly payment shall be based on Party B's discretion. Party A shall not deduct from the payable information fee, the amount of information fee refused to be paid by any users due to Party B's service quality.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the basis for settlement to include unsuccessful charges and removing deductions for refused payments due to service quality contradicts the principles of fair billing and consumer protection. It allows for charging users for services they didn't receive or were dissatisfied with.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 中华人民共和国消费者权益保护法 (Consumer Rights Protection Law of the People's Republic of China)
perturbation.law_citation: Consumer Rights Protection Law of the People's Republic of China, Article 8
perturbation.law_explanation: This change violates Article 8 of the Consumer Rights Protection Law of the People's Republic of China, which guarantees consumers the right to obtain truthful information about goods and services they purchase. Charging for unsuccessful services or refusing to deduct fees for poor service quality is misleading and infringes on consumers' rights to fair transactions. It also goes against Article 16, which states that "Business operators shall provide goods or services to consumers in accordance with the provisions of other relevant laws and regulations or the agreement with consumers; shall perform their obligations, as promised, and shall not provide consumers with goods or services in violation of such provisions or agreement."
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts consumer rights by allowing charges for unsuccessful services and removing deductions for poor service quality, as explained in the Perturbation and Law Explanations. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' definition because the change deviates from legally recognized principles of fair billing and consumer protection.
